faqs.q: What is effect size in research?
faqs.a: Effect size is a number that answers the question of how much. If a study finds a therapy reduces symptoms, the effect size tells you how big that reduction really is, separating whether something worked from how well it worked.

faqs.q: What's the difference between effect size and a p-value?
faqs.a: A p-value speaks to whether an effect is likely real rather than chance, while effect size speaks to how large that effect is. A result can be statistically detectable yet trivially small, so a low p-value alone doesn't mean a finding is important.

faqs.q: Does a large effect size mean a treatment will work for me?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. Effect sizes describe averages across groups, not guarantees for any individual person.
